Gemma family (gen 1 to gen 4) is consistent with extreme range of activations, i.e., 600000, essentially forcing people to use bf16 kv cache and accept a short context window, e.g., 31b, iq4_xs quantization, 100k context window on 32gb memory. Or, people use q8 kv cache, 200k context window, and accept a large performance penalty. In contrast, for qwen 3.5 family, the largest activation is below 2000, making q8 or even lower-precision kv cache essentially free estates.

Building on the "investigation > patch" point — running Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI daily, the pattern I keep noticing is that auto-fix is fine on "obvious bug, obvious fix" (off-by-one, null check, missing await, error not propagated). It falls over on "subtle invariant" bugs where the existing code is intentionally weird to preserve something non-obvious — the PR looks right and breaks something three modules away. The tool I'd actually want isn't "tries harder to fix everything." It's one that credibly says "this touches an invariant I can't see — here's what I think might happen, you handle it." Calibrated humility beats confident patches.
